# FRAMEWORK PDF CAPTIONS — moz-cholera 2026-05-22
# framework_doc: https://www.unocha.org/attachments/b95eb4e9-0a53-4c6c-8243-56d93b8a770f/260522_Mozambique%20cholera%20AA%20framework_final.pdf
# pages: 15 · model: sonnet · via: claude headless
# Auto-generated descriptions of the PDF's visuals (maps/charts/tables). PUBLIC.
------------------------------------------------------------
--- page 1 ---
Cover page and table of contents. Title: "Anticipatory Action Framework Mozambique – Cholera". Approved by RC/HC on 19 May 2026; endorsed by Emergency Relief Coordinator on 22 May 2026. TOC lists sections: Executive Summary, Cholera in Mozambique and anticipatory action, Coordination Arrangements, Trigger Mechanism, Delivery/Anticipatory Action Plan, Pre-arranged Financing, Learning, Annex (pp. 2–15).

--- page 2 ---
Section 1: Executive Summary. Narrative text, no figure. Key points: trigger is epidemiological (district- and province-level AWD surveillance); district threshold = ≥4× previous week's AWD cases OR exceeds province-specific 99th percentile, with minimum 100 (non-capitals) or 300 (capitals) absolute cases, in ≥2 districts of the same province for 3 consecutive weeks; province threshold = >2,500 AWD cases/week for 3 consecutive weeks, plus cholera confirmed/classified as probable. Action plan focuses on four pillars: surveillance & laboratory support, case management/IPC, WASH, risk communication & community engagement. Pre-arranged financing: up to US$1.5 million via CERF; validity period two years; CERF disburses funds once.

--- page 3 ---
Section 1 (continued): Executive Summary continuation. Narrative text, no figure. Covers: coordination through existing national/provincial cholera response mechanisms; RC/HC and ERC endorsement of framework and CERF package; each activation releases US$750,000; partners co-fund; two-year validity period; learning activities including after-action reviews; lessons from DRC cholera AA framework incorporated.

--- page 4 ---
Section 4: Cholera in Mozambique and anticipatory action. Includes a choropleth/dot map of Mozambique showing cholera outbreak distribution. Map shows districts across all provinces with outbreak markers; largest outbreak July 2024 recorded across 93 districts; 48,700+ cases reported in two decades; transmission peaks September–April (rainy season). Key provinces mentioned: Cabo Delgado, Nampula, Sofala, Tete. Bar/trend chart implied by text showing escalation in recent years (2024 outbreak largest in two decades). Main point: cholera is endemic, seasonal, and escalating — justifying anticipatory action.

--- page 5 ---
Section 5: Coordination Arrangements. Narrative text, no figure. Core group: UNICEF, WHO, OCHA, MISAU (Ministry of Health), INS (National Institute of Health), led by RC/HC. Synergies: Mozambique Red Cross developing simplified Early Action Protocol for cholera; complements AA plans for cyclones (OCHA multi-sectoral framework), drought, and floods. Government alignment: framework contributes to National SOPs for AA against cholera.

--- page 6 ---
Section 6: The Trigger Mechanism — Epidemiological surveillance and triggers/thresholds. Narrative text with highlighted trigger criteria box. Trigger is based on weekly AWD surveillance data. District-level threshold (must reach ≥2 districts in same province for 3 consecutive weeks): Weekly AWD cases ≥4× value of previous week OR exceed province-specific 99th percentile; AND in both cases weekly AWD ≥100 (non-capitals) or ≥300 (capitals). Province-level threshold: provincial total >2,500 AWD cases/week for 3 consecutive weeks. AND: cholera outbreak confirmed or classified as probable. Thresholds defined by retrospective analysis of historical AWD surveillance time series, reviewed/recalibrated every two years.

--- page 7 ---
Section 6 (continued): Provincial Level Thresholds table and GTFCC outbreak definitions. Table — Province-specific 99th percentile thresholds (AWD cases per 100,000 population): Cabo Delgado 301.9, Gaza 267.1, Inhambane 127.5, Manica 74.0, Maputo City 226.2, Maputo Province 136.5, Nampula 87.7, Niassa 272.5, Sofala 150.1, Tete 156, Zambezia 135.9. Two definition boxes: PROBABLE cholera outbreak = suspected cases with ≥1 RDT(+) achieves or surpasses a defined threshold (graphic shows thresholds: 1 RDT+ if ≤4 suspected/14 days; 8-13 → 2 RDT+; 14-20 → 3 RDT+; etc.). CONFIRMED cholera outbreak = at least 1 locally acquired confirmed case.

--- page 8 ---
Section 6 (continued): Roles and responsibilities table for trigger monitoring and activation protocol. Table with columns: When / Who / What / How. Rows: Weekly monitoring — WHO — shares AWD surveillance data with CHD — Digital format; When threshold approached — WHO — requests info from CHD that outbreak is confirmed/probable per GTFCC criteria — Email; When trigger met — CHD — notifies RC/HC, OCHA Country Office, Regional Office, HCT, CERF, UNICEF, WHO, MISAU, HCT, ICCG — Email; After notification by CHD — CERF Secretariat — sends approval letters to UN agencies — Email; After approval letters sent — OCHA Mozambique — convenes coordination meeting within 24 hours — Meeting; After coordination meeting — WHO, UNICEF, partners — begin immediate project implementation — As per framework.

--- page 9 ---
Section 7: The Delivery: Anticipatory Action Plan. Narrative text, no figure. Selection criteria for interventions: anticipatory character, ability to deliver within window of opportunity, operational capacity, no regrets engagement. Four priority intervention pillars: (1) Surveillance and laboratory support, (2) Case management/IPC, (3) WASH, (4) Risk Communication and Community Engagement. Coordination via cholera task force and incident management system; task force meets regularly at district level; geographical scope agreed at task force meeting; expansion to neighboring provinces possible with rationale. Timing: all agencies confirm readiness immediately; all activities implemented within three months of activation.

--- page 10 ---
Section 7 (continued): Ensuring readiness for timely implementation. Narrative text, no figure. Agency-specific preparedness activities listed. Risk Communication and Community Engagement (UNICEF/partners): routine data collection on misinformation, regular hygiene promotion, community capacity building. WASH (agencies): preposition emergency supplies, train partners, establish contingency partnerships. WHO (7 items): monitor AWD surveillance data, develop preparedness/response plans, implement cholera readiness assessments with MISAU, grade with IMS activation, support districts on contingency plans, preposition cholera kits, supply provinces.

--- page 11 ---
Section 8: Pre-arranged Financing. Funding by agency table. CERF allocation: US$1,500,000 total. UNICEF (WASH sector): CERF US$1,000,000 + Preparedness co-funding US$100,000 + Implementation co-funding US$150,000 = Total US$1,250,000 (people targeted: not specified in cell). WHO (Health sector): CERF US$500,000 + Preparedness co-funding US$115,000 + Implementation co-funding US$128,000 = Total US$743,000 (people targeted: not specified). TOTAL: CERF US$1,500,000 + Preparedness co-funding US$215,000 + Implementation co-funding US$278,000 = Grand total US$1,993,000. CERF disburses on no-regrets basis once trigger reached; conditions: RC/HC endorses framework and CERF package, ERC endorses same package, pre-agreed trigger is met. Each activation releases US$750,000.

--- page 12 ---
Section 9: Learning. Narrative text, no figure. M&E: each agency uses existing systems; findings shared; collective learning agenda focused on impact, timeliness, cost-effectiveness vs regular response. Lessons from other frameworks: this is the second coordinated AA framework for cholera (first was DRC); lessons incorporated include: provincial-level trigger integration, greater geographical flexibility, strengthened partner awareness, clear coordination mechanisms.

--- page 13 ---
Section 10: Annex — Overview of cholera activities implemented by partners. Detailed table. Columns: Sector/Response pillar / Objective / Implementing organization / Activities. Rows include: Health – Surveillance & laboratory (Early detection, case referral) → WHO: distribution of rapid diagnostic test (RDT) kits, culture sample collection materials, logistical support for referral of samples to provincial labs, deployment of Rapid Response Teams, one-day refresher training for community actors, active case finding and follow-up, distribution of cholera kits and medical supplies. Health – Case Management, IPC (Reducing case fatality rate) → WHO: setup of Cholera Treatment Centers/Units (CTUs/CTCs) and Oral Rehydration Points (ORPs), refresher training for health staff, recruitment of additional staff for case management and IPC in CTCs/CTUs/ORPs, refresher training of community leaders in safe burials, train WASH actors on cholera preparedness, hygiene promotion, rapid response protocols. Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH) – Reducing cholera outbreaks and transmission risk → UNICEF: safe water access activities (monitor/treat community water sources, maintain water supply systems/hand pumps, distribute water purification supplies, train households on proper use); sanitation and hygiene promotion activities (promote construction/repair of household and institutional latrines, safe disposal of human waste, disseminate key messages on handwashing/hygiene, mobilize volunteers/community activists for cholera awareness, improve IPC in health facilities); water quality surveillance activities.

--- page 14 ---
Section 10 (Annex continued): Objectives of anticipatory action and core principles. Infographic: "What does collective anticipatory action look like?" — four icons representing: Robust Forecasting/Model (trigger-based), Escalated Pre-arranged Financing (money), Anticipatory Action (coordinated delivery), Reduced Crisis (outcome). Three core elements: model (forecasting), money (pre-arranged financing), delivery. Core principles listed (9 OCHA principles): multi-sectoral approach, ensuring the continuum of protection, promoting accountability to affected populations, supporting localization, engaging in partnerships, generating co-benefits, capacitating, striving for integration. Table bottom: Average Annual Spending — Provincial 4.7, District 7.0, Combined 2.8; Return Period (Full Envelope) — Provincial 4.7, District 7.0, Combined 2.8; Average Annual Spending per US$50k per activation — Provincial 321,429, District 214,286, Combined 535,714.

--- page 15 ---
Section 10 (Annex continued): Core principles (continued) and Average Annual Spending table. Core principles text: multi-sectoral approach, ensuring continuum of protection, promoting accountability to affected populations, supporting localization, engaging in partnerships, generating co-benefits, capacitating (Strengthening for Integration). Average Annual Spending table: Return Period (Full Envelope) — Provincial 4.7 years, District 7.0 years, Combined 2.8 years; Average Annual Spending (US$ per activation) — Provincial 321,429, District 214,286, Combined 535,714.
